aliases: Grilled Eel Rice Bowl Double Portion

english: Grilled Eel Rice Bowl Double Portion

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn does not have a specific unadon entry; 'eel dish' (FOODON_03311737) is the closest defensible parent class. The double-portion modifier is not captured in FoodOn.
ontology.foodex2_description: Eel, freshwater, grilled — used as the primary ingredient anchor for this composite dish
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A00GJ covers grilled freshwater eel as an ingredient; no composite unadon dish code exists. Rice component and sauce are not separately coded here.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code exists for unadon or grilled eel rice bowl as a composite dish.

main_ingredients: Unagi tare sauce (soy sauce, mirin, sake, sugar)

serving.natural_language_portion: One double-portion unagi plate weighing approximately 550 g, comprising a larger bed of steamed Japanese short-grain rice topped with two fillets of grilled freshwater eel glazed with sweet-savoury tare sauce.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Eel, mixed species, cooked, dry heat (FDC ID 175136) combined with Cooked white short-grain rice (FDC ID 169756) and estimated tare sauce contribution
nutrient_source.organization: U.S. Department of Agriculture, Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single approved-source entry exists for a composite unadon dish. Nutrient values are estimated by combining USDA FoodData Central data for grilled eel (FDC ID 175136, ~45% by weight) and cooked Japanese short-grain white rice (FDC ID 169756, ~50% by weight) with an estimated tare sauce contribution (~5% by weight) for sodium and sugar. The input record's per-100g values (energy 162.5 kcal, protein 8.75 g, fat 7.5 g, carbohydrate 15.0 g) are broadly consistent with this weighted blend and have been retained with minor cross-validation adjustments. Monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, and several micronutrients are set to -1 where USDA data for the composite blend is not directly available.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC; white rice (Japanese, short-grain, cooked) GI ~72
gi_source.organization: Diabetes Care, American Diabetes Association (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No GI value exists for a composite unadon dish in the University of Sydney GI Database. The GI of 72 is taken from the peer-reviewed Atkinson et al. (2008) international GI tables for cooked Japanese short-grain white rice, which constitutes approximately 50% of the dish by weight and is the dominant carbohydrate driver. The eel and tare sauce components contribute negligible additional carbohydrate. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GL = (GI × carbohydrate_g_per_serving) / 100 = (72 × 82.5) / 100 = 59.4, where carbohydrate per 550 g serving = 15.0 × 5.5 = 82.5 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 300 mg/100 g; a 550 g double-portion delivers approximately 1,650 mg sodium — 71% of the WHO 2,300 mg daily limit in a single meal
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should request reduced or no tare sauce to substantially lower sodium intake, avoid additional condiments such as pickled ginger (gari), and limit consumption to a single standard portion rather than the double portion.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High-GI Japanese short-grain rice (GI 72) with 82.5 g carbohydrate per double-portion serving and only 2.75 g dietary fibre drives a glycemic load of approximately 59, well above the high-GL threshold of 20
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should avoid the double portion, request brown rice if available to lower the glycemic response, ask for reduced tare sauce to minimise added sugar from mirin, and pair the meal with non-starchy vegetables to slow glucose absorption.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Unagi provides cardioprotective omega-3 fatty acids (EPA/DHA) but the double portion delivers approximately 41.25 g total fat and 11 g saturated fat, and 1,650 mg sodium — all of which are unfavourable for cardiovascular risk management
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease may benefit from the omega-3 content of unagi but should limit to a single standard portion to control total fat, saturated fat, and sodium intake, and request reduced tare sauce to lower sodium and added sugar.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Unagi is a source of anti-inflammatory omega-3 fatty acids (EPA/DHA); however, the sweet tare sauce adds sugar which promotes pro-inflammatory mediators at high intake
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: A single portion with reduced tare sauce is preferable; the omega-3 content of unagi supports joint inflammation reduction, but excess sugar and sodium in the double portion may offset this benefit.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Unagi Rice Bowl Double Portion (うな丼 大盛り, Unadon ōmori) is a Japanese dish consisting of two fillets of grilled freshwater eel (unagi, Anguilla japonica) glazed with a sweet-savoury tare sauce — made from soy sauce, mirin, sake, and sugar — served over a generous double portion of steamed Japanese short-grain white rice, typically garnished with sansho pepper. The double-portion serving weighs approximately 550 g. Unagi is grilled using the kabayaki technique: the eel is split, skewered, grilled, steamed to remove excess fat, then re-grilled and repeatedly basted with tare sauce to develop a lacquered, caramelised exterior with a tender, rich interior. The flavour profile is deeply savoury and sweet with a slight smokiness; the texture is soft and yielding. Per 100 g the dish provides approximately 162.5 kcal, 8.75 g protein, 7.5 g fat (2.0 g saturated), and 15.0 g carbohydrate with 3.75 g sugar and 300 mg sodium. The double portion delivers roughly 894 kcal, 48 g protein, 41 g fat, and 82.5 g carbohydrate in a single meal. Clinically, the dish is high in sodium (approximately 1,650 mg per double portion) and has a high glycemic load (~59) driven by the refined short-grain rice, making it unsuitable for individuals managing hypertension or Type 2 Diabetes without significant modification. The omega-3 fatty acid content of unagi offers cardiovascular and anti-inflammatory benefits. This dish is commonly served in Japanese restaurants and Japanese food stalls in Singapore.
common_user_queries: How many calories in a double portion unagi plate?
Is grilled eel rice bowl high in carbs?
Can I eat unagi don with high blood pressure?

confidence_reason: No single approved-source entry exists for a composite unadon dish. Nutrient values are estimated by blending USDA FoodData Central data for grilled eel (FDC ID 175136) and cooked Japanese short-grain white rice (FDC ID 169756) with an estimated tare sauce contribution. The input record values are broadly consistent with this blend and have been retained. GI is estimated from the peer-reviewed Atkinson et al. (2008) international GI tables using Japanese short-grain white rice as the dominant carbohydrate source. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: 1. monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractionation data for a composite unadon dish (grilled eel + rice + tare sauce) is not directly available in USDA FoodData Central or the Singapore HPB database as a single composite entry. USDA FDC ID 175136 (eel, cooked, dry heat) reports MUFA ~4.5 g/100g and PUFA ~1.1 g/100g for pure eel; however, these cannot be reliably apportioned to the composite dish without validated recipe weights, so -1 is used to avoid false precision. 2. potassium_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: composite dish micronutrient data is not available in a single approved-source entry; individual ingredient data from USDA FDC exists but cannot be reliably blended without validated portion weights for this specific restaurant preparation. 3. trans_fat_g is 0.0: grilled freshwater eel and plain steamed rice do not naturally contain industrially produced trans fats; confirmed negligible per USDA FDC data for both components. 4. vitamin_c_mg is 0.0: neither eel nor plain white rice contains meaningful vitamin C; confirmed negligible per USDA FDC. 5. The input record listed two portions both flagged is_default=true (400 g and 550 g); the 550 g double-portion value is used as the serving size for this record as it is the named dish. 6. The input source URL (https://foodo.sg/) was not used as a nutrient source as it is not an approved primary nutrient source under Project Veritas v2 source policy; USDA FoodData Central is used instead. 7. FoodOn match FOODON_03311737 ('eel dish') is approximate; no exact unadon composite dish URI was found in FoodOn as of 2024. 8. FoodEx2 code A00GJ is used as the primary ingredient anchor for grilled freshwater eel; no composite unadon dish code exists in FoodEx2.

Added mandatory local names for Chinese (simplified and traditional), Malay, Tamil, and Hindi to local_names and aliases_by_language, as the dish is served in Singapore and the scoring model requires these for Singaporean dishes. Also added these to the flat aliases array.
